text:
RPD File No. / N° de dossier de la SPR : TA9-06911
Private Proceeding / Huis clos
Reasons and Decision ? Motifs et décision
Claimant(s)
XXXX XXXX
Demandeur(e)(s) d'asile
Date(s) of Hearing
June 6, 2012
Date(s) de l'audience
Place of Hearing
Toronto, Ontario
Lieu de l'audience
Date of Decision
August 2, 2012
Date de la décision

Mamerto Geronimo
Tribunal
Counsel for the Claimant(s)
Arthur I. Yallen
(Barrister and Solicitor)
Conseil(s) du / de la / des demandeur(e)(s) d'asile
Tribunal Officer
N/A
Agent(e) de tribunal

N/A
Représentant(e)(s) désigné(e)(s)

N/A
Conseil du ministre
[1] XXXX XXXX claims protection pursuant to sections 96 and 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act1 (IRPA).
SUMMARY OF ALLEGED FACTS
[2] The specifics of the claim are set out in the narrative to the claimant's Personal Information Form2 (PIF) and the claimants' oral testimony. The following is a summary:
* The claimant resided in the town of XXXX in Ukraine and is a citizen of Ukraine.
* Her father is of Ukrainian nationality while her mother is Jewish.
* She was subjected to insult, discrimination, harassment and physical abuse/harm in Ukraine as a result of her Jewish nationality.
* She reported these anti-Semitic incidents to the police but the police ignored her complaints.
* Fearing for her life, she left Ukraine, arrived in Canada on XXXX XXXX, 2009, and filed her refugee claim on March 30, 2009.
DETERMINATION
[3] I find the claimant not a Convention refugee pursuant to section 96 of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act. I also find that, on a balance of probabilities, the claimant is not a person in need of protection under section 97 (1)(b) of the IRPA.
ANALYSIS
Identity
[4] I find the claimant a citizen of Ukraine. This has been established by prima facie evidence of her Ukrainian passport and her Canadian visitor's visa.3
Credibility
[5] The claimant did not provide sufficient credible evidence to support the allegations of her claim.
[6] The claimant gave documentary evidence that she lived in the town of XXXX, from 1999 to 2008.4 She testified that it is such a small community that everybody knew that she was Jewish (emphasis added). She stated in the narrative to her PIF that she lived in fear her whole life, her neighbors often insulted her and that she was so afraid of them that she would not even go out during any celebration of Christmas holidays.5
[7] The claimant gave oral testimony as to her fears in Ukraine.
Q. Who do you fear in Ukraine?
A. The nationalists and the anti-Semites.
Q. When did your problem(s) start in Ukraine?
A. From 2008.
Q. What about in 2005?
A. Not that great extent.
Q. Did your problems with your neighbours and nationalists in 2006 continue till you moved to XXXX in XXXX 2008?
A. Yes.
Q. Why did you wait for two (2) years to move to XXXX?
A. I did not think it would escalate.
[8] The claimant's evidence of her subjective fear is saddled with inconsistencies that it is deserving of very little weight or no weight at all. She claimed that she feared for her life in Ukraine, as everybody in her place of residence knew that she was Jewish, yet she lived in her small community in the town of XXXX for nine (9) years (1999 to 2008) until she moved to another city, XXXX. If she was truly known as a Jew in her town and anti-Semitism truly posed a risk to her life, common sense and reason dictate that it is not believable that she was able to reside and stay in her town for those long nine (9) years. Either her claim of being a Jew, or being perceived to be a Jew, or the alleged peril to her life at the hands of anti-Semites is not true. Her explanation that she waited for two (2) years to move to XXXX after she was attacked by carollers in 2006 because she did not think that it (persecution) would continue or escalate belies her accounts of threats, vandalism and physical assaults inflicted on her by her neighbours and other anti-Semitic people in 2006, 2007 and up to the middle of 2008. She even provided a police report for an incident in 2005. Given the panel's credibility concerns, and given that her two sisters still reside in XXXX and that her sister obtained and sent her the police report, the likelihood of her being cruelly beaten and kicked in the stomach on more than one occasion by her neighbours and other anti-Semitic people at her age of 69 years, is, on a balance of probabilities, of dubious occurrence. The claimant submitted documentary evidence in terms of a medical certificate and a police report to support the physical violence inflicted on her as a consequence of her Jewish nationality. When asked as to which incident in her narrative the police report6 referred to, the claimant stated that it was briefly mentioned in her narrative. The panel and counsel reviewed the narrative and counsel admitted that there was no incident mentioned in the claimant's narrative in reference to the police report. Consequently, no probative value is accorded to the police report. When asked for a copy of the hospital report where she was brought for treatment after she was seriously beaten up by her neighbour in August 2008, she testified that although she requested it, the hospital staff refused to issue one. Oddly enough, the claimant submitted a hospital report7 from the same hospital for the treatment of her injuries sustained in XXXX 2008. The claimant testified that there was only one hospital in the town of XXXX and offered no explanation as to why she was refused a medical report in XXXX 2008 and was issued one for another incident two (2) months later. The RPD is not required to point out all of the irregularities, nor every aspect of implausibility in the applicant's testimony, nor has the obligation to confront the applicant with all of the inconsistencies at the hearing, particularly since she was represented by counsel.8
[9] A close review of the medical and police reports shows that both documents do not have printed letterheads of the hospital and the Ministry of Internal Affairs. Instead, a rectangular (rubber) stamp was used to show the source of the documents. When asked for an explanation, the claimant testified that it was the standard form issued in her community in the town of XXXX. While it may not be determinative of the authenticity of the documents, for a hospital and a government office not to have official and printed letterheads, raises a serious credibility concern as to the legitimacy of these documents. The Board's country conditions documentation confirms,
Ukraine: Availability of fraudulent or illegally obtained medical and police reports (February 2006). According to the director of the Kharkov Human Rights Protection Group (KHPG), a non-governmental organization based in Kharkov, Ukraine, it is possible to obtain fraudulent medical and police reports throughout Ukraine. This source explained that police officials and hospital staff in Ukraine earn a low salary and some are therefore willing to accept money to produce such fraudulent documents. This source further added that people can obtain such reports by making a contact in the police force or medical establishment who will then locate another individual in those institutions to produce such documents for them.
An associate professor of History and Political Science at the University of Western Ontario, who specializes in Ukraine and who lived and worked there for several years as a journalist, corroborated this information in 25 January 2006 correspondence to the Research Directorate. This source wrote that although it is impossible to provide accurate statistics on such illegal activity, the prevalence of fraudulent medical and police reports in Ukraine is "quite widespread" (25 Jan. 2006). Although lacking personal experience in such activity, this source stated that such documents are obtained by "discreetly" inquiring about the drawing up of such reports, finding someone who can organize their printing, and then "a price is quoted [and...] arrangements are made for payment delivery." If a police report is required, then one usually looks for some contacts in the police department.9
[10] In my view, on a balance of probabilities, the medical and police reports are from dubious sources and deserve no weight at all. The case law establishes that assessing the evidence and the testimony, as well as determining the probative value to be attached to these, falls within the Immigration and Refugee Board's purview.10 Given this finding, I draw a negative inference and cast doubt upon the totality of the claimant's evidence.
State Protection
[11] The claimant did not provide clear and convincing evidence to rebut the presumption of state protection.
[12] The claimant gave evidence that she made several complaints to the police as to the anti-Semitic acts inflicted on her but she did not receive any assistance/protection at all. When asked if she considered or did approach higher police authorities or other government agencies, like the prosecutor's office, to follow up on her complaint, she testified that she did not and even if she did, her complaint would be sent back to the local authorities to look into the matter. When asked how she knew that would happen, she stated that it happened before with other people. Upon questioning, she testified that she did not have personal knowledge but heard about these within their small town. The fact that the claimant made no attempts to seek assistance from higher police authorities or other government agencies does not constitute an adequate search for state protection, especially in a democratic state, just because certain police officers refused to intervene or she heard that her complaint would just be referred back to the same local police officer/station.11 The evidence provided does not establish that making additional attempts to obtain state protection would have been objectively unreasonable. It could be said that the claimant did not diligently seek protection from her country of origin. It cannot automatically be concluded that a democratic state is incapable of protecting one of its nationals because certain police officers refused to intervene.12 No state can guarantee perfect protection.13 Where a state is in effective control of its territory and makes serious efforts to protect its citizens, the mere fact that the state's efforts are not always successful will not rebut the presumption of state protection.14 The burden of proving the ineffectiveness of state protection lies with the claimant. The claimant did not do so.
[13] Sadly, anti-Semitism is experienced all over the world, including Canada.15 The claimant's documentary evidence on anti-Semitism in Ukraine (including her post-hearing disclosure) reports societal and anti-Semitic abuses, discrimination and violent acts against persons and property in the Ukraine. The Board's country documentation on anti-Semitism in the Ukraine16 also confirms:
There was some violence and discrimination against women and children, and reports of non - violent incidents of anti-Semitism.
Anti-Semitism
There were a number of acts of anti-Semitism, some involving vandalism of Jewish property. According to the Association of Jewish Organizations and Communities of Ukraine's (VAAD) there were nine incidents of vandalism during the year compared with 19 incidents in 2009, and 13 in 2008. There were no reports of violent incidents of anti-Semitism.
During the year, members of marginal populist and nationalist parties and organizations continued to make occasional extremist, intolerant, and anti-Semitic statements.
On September 10, the Prosecutor's Office in Zakarpattia closed an investigation into charges of hate speech against Serhiy Ratushnyak, the former mayor of Uzhhorod. Citing findings by legal and linguistic experts, the prosecutor stated Ratushnyak's comments made in 2009 reflected his opinion about Jews and could not be described as hate speech. Ratushnyak was charged in August 2009 with inciting ethnic hatred, hooliganism, and abuse of office after he allegedly used anti-Semitic rhetoric and attacked a campaign worker for a rival presidential candidate. Ratushnyak, who was known for making racist and intolerant comments, ran as a marginal candidate in the presidential elections.
The Kherson Oblast Prosecutor's Office also opened a criminal case against Kyrychenko on charges of inciting interethnic hatred. In December the prosecutor completed a pretrial investigation and sent the case to court.
Anti-Semitic articles continued to appear in small publications, although their number and circulation continued to decline. According to VAAD, 46 anti-Semitic articles were published in major print media outlets in 2009, compared with 54 in 2008 and 542 in 2007.
VAAD said the sharp decrease in anti-Semitic publications was due primarily to concerted political and social pressure by NGOs, the government and the Jewish community on the Academy of Personnel Management (MAUP). In previous years, MAUP, a private higher-education institution, accounted for nearly 90 percent of all anti-Semitic material, but has now ceased the publications.
Senior government officials and politicians from various political parties continued efforts to combat anti-Semitism by speaking out against extremism and social intolerance, and by criticizing anti-Semitic acts.
The SCNR, together with the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, Ministry of Internal Affairs, State Border Guard Committee, State Customs Service, State Committee for Tourism, and other agencies, cooperated to support Jewish pilgrimages to the burial site in Uman of Rabbi Nakhman, founder of the Bratslav Hasidic movement. According to Jewish leaders approximately 23,000 pilgrims traveled to Uman in September. Growing numbers of Jewish pilgrims have been visiting burial sites of prominent spiritual leaders in Medzhybizh, Berdychiv, and Hadyach.
On August 4, an embassy officer attended a candle-lighting ceremony at the Jewish cemetery in Lviv. Local and regional government officials also attended. The officer stressed the U.S. government's support for religious freedom and minority rights in the country.
Embassy officers continued to maintain close contact not only with clerics but also with lay leaders in religious communities and representatives of faith-based social service organizations, such as Caritas, the American-Jewish Joint Distribution Committee, and the National Conference on Soviet Jewry. The embassy continued to intervene as necessary to defend foreign religious workers' rights to due process under the law.
According to the government the SBU acted to prevent at least six hate crimes in 2009 and 2010, including illegal activities by skinhead groups in Cherkassy and Dnepropetrovsk and an attack on the cultural center Hesed Haim in Sumy.
[14] While any act of hate is abhorrent, the number of anti-Semitic incidents cited in conjunction with the Jewish population of 103,600 and could be as high as 370,000,17 the government's efforts to combat anti-Semitism and the prosecution of anti-Semitic activities and most importantly, the panel's credibility concerns on the claimant's allegations and her documentary evidence (medical and police reports) do not result in a serious possibility that the claimant would be persecuted or that she would be subjected, on a balance of probabilities, to a risk to life or a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment or a danger of torture, if she were to return to Ukraine.
[15] The claimant's documentary evidence18 reports,
The parliamentary democracy has struggled to develop a state affirming history necessary to build unity and loyalty, and in 2002, MAUP (the Interregional Academy of Personal Management) stepped in and propositioned itself as a torchbearer of Ukraine nationalism.
Veins of anti-Semitism still run deep as they do elsewhere in Europe.
While some older Jews continue to keep mum about their ethnicity, fearing that the government's policy toward Jews could shift, younger Jews are more comfortable "coming out." Says Alisa Linderman, who is from Galicia but lives in Kiev and is in her early 20's: "Jewish teenagers today are not afraid to be Jewish. If they hear something anti-Semitic, they want answers.
Her friend Vika Dihne, 22, is a Jewish MAUP graduate working for the Israel Cultural Center at the Israeli embassy in Kiev, recruiting young Ukrainians for Birthright and programs that make it possible for them to visit Israel. "We advertise in newspapers and by word of mouth and it's getting easier." She sees much excitement about Jews and Israel. "If I asked people two or three years ago if they have Jewish roots they would say 'No, no, no.' Now they say 'Yes.' People now call the embassy and ask where they can study Hebrew, and they are not even Jewish!"
Dihne says she did not encounter anti-Semitism at MAUP, either in the curriculum or among her teachers and friends, even though her time there coincided with the school's campaign. "There were no people in my group who hated Jews."
But Jed Sunden warns me not to confuse MAUP and the far right-wing with the Ukrainian people of the government. "At the end of the day there's a Jewish community here that is thriving. Modern Ukraine has a phenomenal record." Since 1991, Ukraine has normalized relations with Israel, including two-way immigration trade and tourism. Ukrainian synagogues, schools, and organizations have been established with funds from the West. Some buildings have been reclaimed and renovated, and restaurants and stores catering to Jews have opened. Both Yushchenko and Tymoshenko have traveled to Israel and assured Jews hat they will fight anti-Semitism.
[16] Furthermore, the Board's documentation states,
Ukraine contains the third-largest Jewish community in Europe and the fifth-largest Jewish community in the world. The majority of Ukrainian Jews live in four large cities: Kyiv, Dnipropetrovsk, Kharkiv, and Odessa. Despite the limitations imposed on them by various governments and regimes throughout modern history, Jews played a major role in developing Ukraine's commerce and industry as well as the major urban environments of Ukraine.19
[17] Given the panel's credibility findings and the claimant's failure to rebut the presumption of state protection, I find that, on a balance of probabilities, the claimant is not a person in need of protection under section 97(1) (b). There was also no evidence adduced that would support a finding that the claimant faces a danger of torture under section 97(1) (a).
CONCLUSION
[18] Based on the foregoing analysis and considering the totality of the evidence, I find that there is not a reasonable chance or serious possibility of persecution, if the claimant were to return to Ukraine. I also find that, on a balance of probabilities, she would not be personally subjected to a
risk of torture, a risk to life, or a risk of cruel and unusual treatment or punishment, if she were to return to Ukraine. Accordingly, her claim is rejected.
(signed)
"Mamerto Geronimo"
Mamerto Geronimo
August 2, 2012
Date
